chapterList.description: Chapter 1: Arjuna Vishada Yoga - The Yoga of Arjuna`s Dejection
chapterList.name: Chapter 1: Arjuna Vishada Yoga - The Yoga of Arjuna`s Dejection

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.1: Dhritarashtra said - O Sanjaya, assembled in the holy land of Kurukshetra and desiring to fight, what did my sons and the Pandavas do?
shlokaList.name: Dhritarashtra said - O Sanjaya, assembled in the holy land of Kurukshetra and desiring to fight, what did my sons and the Pandavas do?
shlokaList.sanskritText:
dhṛitarāśhtra uvācha
dharma-kṣhetre kuru-kṣhetre samavetā yuyutsavaḥ
māmakāḥ pāṇḍavāśhchaiva kimakurvata sañjaya
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.1

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.2: Sanjaya said – O King! Having seen the Pandava army standing in order and ready for the war, Duryodhana approached his teacher Drona and spoke these words;
shlokaList.name: Sanjaya said – O King! Having seen the Pandava army standing in order and ready for the war, Duryodhana approached his teacher Drona and spoke these words;
shlokaList.sanskritText:
sañjaya uvācha
dṛiṣhṭvā tu pāṇḍavānīkaṁ vyūḍhaṁ duryodhanastadā
āchāryamupasaṅgamya rājā vachanamabravīt
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.2

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.3: O teacher, behold this mighty army of the sons of Pandu, arrayed by your intelligent disciple, the son of King Drupada.
shlokaList.name: O teacher, behold this mighty army of the sons of Pandu, arrayed by your intelligent disciple, the son of King Drupada.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
paśhyaitāṁ pāṇḍu-putrāṇām āchārya mahatīṁ chamūm
vyūḍhāṁ drupada-putreṇa tava śhiṣhyeṇa dhīmatā
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.3

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.4 (4-6): Here in this army are many heroes, who are mighty archers and are equal in battle to Bhima and Arjuna and great chariot warriors like Yuyudhana, Virata and Drupada.
shlokaList.name: Here in this army are many heroes, who are mighty archers and are equal in battle to Bhima and Arjuna and great chariot warriors like Yuyudhana, Virata and Drupada.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
atra śhūrā maheṣhvāsā bhīmārjuna-samā yudhi
yuyudhāno virāṭaśhcha drupadaśhcha mahā-rathaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.4 (1.4 to 1.6)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.5 (4-6): There are also valiant heroes like Dhrishtaketu, Chekitana, king of Kashi, Purujit, Kuntibhoja, and Shaibya.
shlokaList.name: There are also valiant heroes like Dhrishtaketu, Chekitana, king of Kashi, Purujit, Kuntibhoja, and Shaibya.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
dhṛiṣhṭaketuśhchekitānaḥ kāśhirājaśhcha vīryavān
purujit kuntibhojaśhcha śhaibyaśhcha nara-puṅgavaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.5 (1.4 to 1.6)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.6 (4-6): The valiant Yudhamanyu, the mighty Uttamaujas, the son of Subhadra and the sons of Draupadi—all of them are great chariot-warriors.
shlokaList.name: The valiant Yudhamanyu, the mighty Uttamaujas, the son of Subhadra and the sons of Draupadi—all of them are great chariot-warriors.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yudhāmanyuśhcha vikrānta uttamaujāśhcha vīryavān
saubhadro draupadeyāśhcha sarva eva mahā-rathāḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.6 (1.4 to 1.6)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.7 (7-9): O the best of the Brahamanas, for your knowledge, I now recount the names of those who are most distinguished amongst us and are able leaders in my army.
shlokaList.name: O the best of the Brahamanas, for your knowledge, I now recount the names of those who are most distinguished amongst us and are able leaders in my army.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
asmākaṁ tu viśhiṣhṭā ye tānnibodha dwijottama
nāyakā mama sainyasya sanjñārthaṁ tānbravīmi te
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.7 (1.7 to 1.9)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.8 (7-9): Your good self, Bhishma, Karna, Kripa, Ashwatthama, Vikarna and the son of Somadatta, who always win in battles.
shlokaList.name: Your good self, Bhishma, Karna, Kripa, Ashwatthama, Vikarna and the son of Somadatta, who always win in battles.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
bhavānbhīṣhmaśhcha karṇaśhcha kṛipaśhcha samitiñjayaḥ
aśhvatthāmā vikarṇaśhcha saumadattis tathaiva cha
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.8 (1.7 to 1.9)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.9 (7-9): And there are many other heroes who are ready to give up their lives for my sake, armed with various weapons, all skilled in warfare.
shlokaList.name: And there are many other heroes who are ready to give up their lives for my sake, armed with various weapons, all skilled in warfare.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
anye cha bahavaḥ śhūrā madarthe tyaktajīvitāḥ
nānā-śhastra-praharaṇāḥ sarve yuddha-viśhāradāḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.9 (1.7 to 1.9)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.10: Our army, completely protected by the great warrior Bhishma is unlimited in strength, while their army carefully secured by Bhima is limited in strength.
shlokaList.name: Our army, completely protected by the great warrior Bhishma is unlimited in strength, while their army carefully secured by Bhima is limited in strength.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
aparyāptaṁ tadasmākaṁ balaṁ bhīṣhmābhirakṣhitam
paryāptaṁ tvidameteṣhāṁ balaṁ bhīmābhirakṣhitam
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.10

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.11: Therefore I summon all the generals of the Kaurava army to extend unwavering support to great Bhishma while diligently safeguarding your designated strategic positions.
shlokaList.name: Therefore I summon all the generals of the Kaurava army to extend unwavering support to great Bhishma while diligently safeguarding your designated strategic positions.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
ayaneṣhu cha sarveṣhu yathā-bhāgamavasthitāḥ
bhīṣhmamevābhirakṣhantu bhavantaḥ sarva eva hi
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.11

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.12 (12-13): Sanjaya said - Thereafter the venerable elder of the Kuru dynasty, the illustrious patriarch Bhishma let out a resounding roar akin to a lion by sound in his conch shell with great vigour bringing delight to Duryodhana.
shlokaList.name: Sanjaya said - Thereafter the venerable elder of the Kuru dynasty, the illustrious patriarch Bhishma let out a resounding roar akin to a lion by sound in his conch shell with great vigour bringing delight to Duryodhana.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
tasya sañjanayan harṣhaṁ kuru-vṛiddhaḥ pitāmahaḥ
siṁha-nādaṁ vinadyochchaiḥ śhaṅkhaṁ dadhmau pratāpavān
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.12 (1.12 to 1.13)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.13 (12-13): Then numerous conches, kettledrums, bugles, trumpets and horns suddenly blared forth and their collective sound was tremendous.
shlokaList.name: Then numerous conches, kettledrums, bugles, trumpets and horns suddenly blared forth and their collective sound was tremendous.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
tataḥ śhaṅkhāśhcha bheryaśhcha paṇavānaka-gomukhāḥ
sahasaivābhyahanyanta sa śhabdastumulo ’bhavat
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.13 (1.12 to 1.13)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.14 (14-18): There upon from the Pandava army, seated on a magnificent chariot pulled by white horses, Madhava (Sri Krishna) and Arjuna resounded their celestial conch shells.
shlokaList.name: There upon from the Pandava army, seated on a magnificent chariot pulled by white horses, Madhava (Sri Krishna) and Arjuna resounded their celestial conch shells.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
tataḥ śhvetairhayairyukte mahati syandane sthitau
mādhavaḥ pāṇḍavaśhchaiva divyau śhaṅkhau pradadhmatuḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.14 (1.14 to 1.18)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.15 (14-18): Lord Krishna, who is also called Hrishikesh sounded his conch named Panchajanya while Arjuna echoed his - the Devadutta. Bhim, renowned for his voracious appetite and extraordinary feats, resounded his powerful conch named Paundra.
shlokaList.name: Lord Krishna, who is also called Hrishikesh sounded his conch named Panchajanya while Arjuna echoed his - the Devadutta. Bhim, renowned for his voracious appetite and extraordinary feats, resounded his powerful conch named Paundra.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
pāñchajanyaṁ hṛiṣhīkeśho devadattaṁ dhanañjayaḥ
pauṇḍraṁ dadhmau mahā-śhaṅkhaṁ bhīma-karmā vṛikodaraḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.15 (1.14 to 1.18)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.16 (14-18): The son of Kunti, king Yudhishthira blew his conch Anantavijaya. Nakula and Sahadeva played their conches Sughoṣa and Maṇipuṣpaka respectively.
shlokaList.name: The son of Kunti, king Yudhishthira blew his conch Anantavijaya. Nakula and Sahadeva played their conches Sughoṣa and Maṇipuṣpaka respectively.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
anantavijayaṁ rājā kuntī-putro yudhiṣhṭhiraḥ
nakulaḥ sahadevaśhcha sughoṣha-maṇipuṣhpakau
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.16 (1.14 to 1.18)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.17 (14-18): The great archer the king of Kashi, the great warrior Shikhandi, Dhrishtadyumna, Viraṭa and the unbeatable Satyaki;
shlokaList.name: The great archer the king of Kashi, the great warrior Shikhandi, Dhrishtadyumna, Viraṭa and the unbeatable Satyaki;
shlokaList.sanskritText:
kāśhyaśhcha parameṣhvāsaḥ śhikhaṇḍī cha mahā-rathaḥ
dhṛiṣhṭadyumno virāṭaśhcha sātyakiśh chāparājitaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.17 (1.14 to 1.18)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.18 (14-18): O king! Drupada and the sons of Draupadi, the mighty armed son of Subhadra, they all sounded their individual conches.
shlokaList.name: O king! Drupada and the sons of Draupadi, the mighty armed son of Subhadra, they all sounded their individual conches.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
drupado draupadeyāśhcha sarvaśhaḥ pṛithivī-pate
saubhadraśhcha mahā-bāhuḥ śhaṅkhāndadhmuḥ pṛithak pṛithak
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.18 (1.14 to 1.18)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.19: The collective sound was tumultuous, reverberating through the sky and the universe, it pierced the hearts of the sons of Dhritarashtra.
shlokaList.name: The collective sound was tumultuous, reverberating through the sky and the universe, it pierced the hearts of the sons of Dhritarashtra.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
sa ghoṣho dhārtarāṣhṭrāṇāṁ hṛidayāni vyadārayat
nabhaśhcha pṛithivīṁ chaiva tumulo abhyanunādayan
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.19

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.20: O King! Then Arjuna, the son of Pandu, whose chariot flag bears the mark of Hanuman, took up his bow to shoot his arrows. As he looked at the army of sons of Dhritrashtra arranged for battle, he spoke the following words to Hrishikesh;
shlokaList.name: O King! Then Arjuna, the son of Pandu, whose chariot flag bears the mark of Hanuman, took up his bow to shoot his arrows. As he looked at the army of sons of Dhritrashtra arranged for battle, he spoke the following words to Hrishikesh;
shlokaList.sanskritText:
atha vyavasthitān dṛiṣhṭvā dhārtarāṣhṭrān kapi-dhwajaḥ
pravṛitte śhastra-sampāte dhanurudyamya pāṇḍavaḥ
hṛiṣhīkeśhaṁ tadā vākyam idam āha mahī-pate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.20

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.21 (21-22): Arjuna said:  O the infallible Lord! Kindly position my chariot in the middle of the two armies. So that I could take a fine look at these men standing here eager to fight;
shlokaList.name: Arjuna said: O the infallible Lord! Kindly position my chariot in the middle of the two armies. So that I could take a fine look at these men standing here eager to fight;
shlokaList.sanskritText:
Arjuna uvācha
senayor ubhayor madhye rathaṁ sthāpaya me ’chyuta
yāvadetān nirīkṣhe ’haṁ yoddhu-kāmān avasthitān
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.21 (1.21 to 1.22)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.22 (21-22): And with whom I have to engage fiercely in this impending war.
shlokaList.name: And with whom I have to engage fiercely in this impending war.
shlokaList.sanskritText: kairmayā saha yoddhavyam asmin raṇa-samudyame
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.22 (1.21 to 1.22)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.23: : I also wish to see those who have come here to fight as well wishers of the malevolent sons of Dhritarashtra.
shlokaList.name: I also wish to see those who have come here to fight as well wishers of the malevolent sons of Dhritarashtra.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yotsyamānān avekṣhe ’haṁ ya ete ’tra samāgatāḥ
dhārtarāṣhṭrasya durbuddher yuddhe priya-chikīrṣhavaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.23

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.24 (24-25): Sanjaya said - O scion of Bharata! As requested by Arjuna, Lord Hrishikesh steered the fine chariot between the two armies.
shlokaList.name: Sanjaya said - O scion of Bharata! As requested by Arjuna, Lord Hrishikesh steered the fine chariot between the two armies.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
sañjaya uvācha
evam ukto hṛiṣhīkeśho guḍākeśhena bhārata
senayor ubhayor madhye sthāpayitvā rathottamam
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.24 (1.24 to 1.25)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.25 (24-25): After placing the chariot in front of Bhishma, Drona and all the dignified kings Lord Krishna said “O Partha! Witness all these Kurus gathered here.”
shlokaList.name: After placing the chariot in front of Bhishma, Drona and all the dignified kings Lord Krishna said “O Partha! Witness all these Kurus gathered here.”
shlokaList.sanskritText:
bhīṣhma-droṇa-pramukhataḥ sarveṣhāṁ cha mahī-kṣhitām
uvācha pārtha paśhyaitān samavetān kurūn iti
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.25 (1.24 to 1.25)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.26 (26-27): There Arjuna saw his uncles, grandfathers, teachers, maternal uncles, brothers, sons and grandsons as well as his mates standing in the ranks. He also saw fathers-in-law and his friends standing in both the opposing armies.
shlokaList.name: There Arjuna saw his uncles, grandfathers, teachers, maternal uncles, brothers, sons and grandsons as well as his mates standing in the ranks. He also saw fathers-in-law and his friends standing in both the opposing armies.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
tatrāpaśhyat sthitān pārthaḥ pitṝīn atha pitāmahān
āchāryān mātulān bhrātṝīn putrān pautrān sakhīṁs tathā
śhvaśhurān suhṛidaśh chaiva senayor ubhayor api
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.26 (1.26 to 1.27)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.27 (26-27): When the son of Kunti (Arjuna) saw all the friends and family members on opposing side he was overcome with extreme emotion and thus spoke these words out of sadness;
shlokaList.name: When the son of Kunti (Arjuna) saw all the friends and family members on opposing side he was overcome with extreme emotion and thus spoke these words out of sadness;
shlokaList.sanskritText:
tān samīkṣhya sa kaunteyaḥ sarvān bandhūn avasthitān
kṛipayā parayāviṣhṭo viṣhīdann idam abravīt
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.27 (1.26 to 1.27)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.28 (28-29): Arjuna said - Looking at these people who are my kin gathered here poised to fight against each other in the battle, O Krishna, my limbs have started to shake and my mouth is getting parched.
shlokaList.name: Arjuna said - Looking at these people who are my kin gathered here poised to fight against each other in the battle, O Krishna, my limbs have started to shake and my mouth is getting parched.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
arjuna uvācha
dṛiṣhṭvemaṁ sva-janaṁ kṛiṣhṇa yuyutsuṁ samupasthitam
sīdanti mama gātrāṇi mukhaṁ cha pariśhuṣhyati
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.28 (1.28 to 1.29)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.29 (28-29): My body is trembling and my body hairs are standing on end, my Gandiva (bow) is slipping from my hand and my skin is burning all over.
shlokaList.name: My body is trembling and my body hairs are standing on end, my Gandiva (bow) is slipping from my hand and my skin is burning all over.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
vepathuśh cha śharīre me roma-harṣhaśh cha jāyate
gāṇḍīvaṁ sraṁsate hastāt tvak chaiva paridahyate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.29 (1.28 to 1.29)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.30: I am failing to stand still and my mind is wandering away. O Keshava! I only see ominous signs of misfortune.
shlokaList.name: I am failing to stand still and my mind is wandering away. O Keshava! I only see ominous signs of misfortune.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
na cha śhaknomy avasthātuṁ bhramatīva cha me manaḥ
nimittāni cha paśhyāmi viparītāni keśhava
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.30

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.31: I do not foresee any good coming out of killing my own kinsmen. O Krishna! I do not seek victory neither do I want the kingdom or any happiness from this war.
shlokaList.name: I do not foresee any good coming out of killing my own kinsmen. O Krishna! I do not seek victory neither do I want the kingdom or any happiness from this war.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
na cha śhreyo ’nupaśhyāmi hatvā sva-janam āhave
na kāṅkṣhe vijayaṁ kṛiṣhṇa na cha rājyaṁ sukhāni cha
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.31

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.32 (32-35): What is the use of such a kingdom, material pleasures or even this life? When those for whose sake we desire kingdom, enjoyment and happiness;
shlokaList.name: What is the use of such a kingdom, material pleasures or even this life? When those for whose sake we desire kingdom, enjoyment and happiness;
shlokaList.sanskritText:
kiṁ no rājyena govinda kiṁ bhogair jīvitena vā
yeṣhām arthe kāṅkṣhitaṁ no rājyaṁ bhogāḥ sukhāni cha
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.32 (1.32 to 1.35)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.33 (32-35):  When they are standing here on the battlefield, risking their wealth and life itself (Among them are) Teachers, fathers, sons as well as grandfathers.
shlokaList.name: When they are standing here on the battlefield, risking their wealth and life itself (Among them are) Teachers, fathers, sons as well as grandfathers.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
ta ime ’vasthitā yuddhe prāṇāṁs tyaktvā dhanāni cha
āchāryāḥ pitaraḥ putrās tathaiva cha pitāmahāḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.33 (1.32 to 1.35)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.34 (32-35): Maternal uncles, fathers-in-law, grandsons, brothers-in-law and other kinsmen are present here, O Madhusudana! I do not want to kill them even if I get killed by them.
shlokaList.name: Maternal uncles, fathers-in-law, grandsons, brothers-in-law and other kinsmen are present here, O Madhusudana! I do not want to kill them even if I get killed by them.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
mātulāḥ śhvaśhurāḥ pautrāḥ śhyālāḥ sambandhinas tathā
etān na hantum ichchhāmi ghnato ’pi madhusūdana
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.34 (1.32 to 1.35)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.35 (32-35): I will not kill my own relatives even if I am offered the reign over three worlds then how can I do such a thing for this kingdom on earth? O Janardana! What satisfaction can we achieve by killing the sons of Dhritarashtra?
shlokaList.name: I will not kill my own relatives even if I am offered the reign over three worlds then how can I do such a thing for this kingdom on earth? O Janardana! What satisfaction can we achieve by killing the sons of Dhritarashtra?
shlokaList.sanskritText:
api trailokya-rājyasya hetoḥ kiṁ nu mahī-kṛite
nihatya dhārtarāṣhṭrān naḥ kā prītiḥ syāj janārdana
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.35 (1.32 to 1.35)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.36: Only sin will befall upon us if we slay these warmongers. O Madhava! Therefore it would not be righteous for us to kill the sons of Dhritarashtra and our other kinsmen. How could we find happiness by murdering our own relatives?
shlokaList.name: Only sin will befall upon us if we slay these warmongers. O Madhava! Therefore it would not be righteous for us to kill the sons of Dhritarashtra and our other kinsmen. How could we find happiness by murdering our own relatives?
shlokaList.sanskritText:
pāpam evāśhrayed asmān hatvaitān ātatāyinaḥ
tasmān nārhā vayaṁ hantuṁ dhārtarāṣhṭrān sa-bāndhavān
sva-janaṁ hi kathaṁ hatvā sukhinaḥ syāma mādhava
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.36

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.37 (37-38): Even though these people, whose minds are overtaken by greed, do not see any wrong in killing their own family or going against their friends.
shlokaList.name: Even though these people, whose minds are overtaken by greed, do not see any wrong in killing their own family or going against their friends.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yady apy ete na paśhyanti lobhopahata-chetasaḥ
kula-kṣhaya-kṛitaṁ doṣhaṁ mitra-drohe cha pātakam
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.37 (1.37 to 1.38)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.38 (37-38): O Janardana! Why are we, who can understand the fault in the destruction of a dynasty, not refrain from engaging in these acts of sin?
shlokaList.name: O Janardana! Why are we, who can understand the fault in the destruction of a dynasty, not refrain from engaging in these acts of sin?
shlokaList.sanskritText:
kathaṁ na jñeyam asmābhiḥ pāpād asmān nivartitum
kula-kṣhaya-kṛitaṁ doṣhaṁ prapaśhyadbhir janārdana
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.38 (1.37 to 1.38)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.39 (39-42): With the destruction of a family, old family traditions also get destroyed; this leads to the destruction of dharma and adharma overwhelms the entire family.
shlokaList.name: With the destruction of a family, old family traditions also get destroyed; this leads to the destruction of dharma and adharma overwhelms the entire family.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
kula-kṣhaye praṇaśhyanti kula-dharmāḥ sanātanāḥ
dharme naṣhṭe kulaṁ kṛitsnam adharmo ’bhibhavaty uta
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.39 (1.39 to 1.42)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.40 (39-42): When adharma predominates, the women of the family become immoral and from the immorality of womanhood, O Vrsni, uncultured children are born.
shlokaList.name: When adharma predominates, the women of the family become immoral and from the immorality of womanhood, O Vrsni, uncultured children are born.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
adharmābhibhavāt kṛiṣhṇa praduṣhyanti kula-striyaḥ
strīṣhu duṣhṭāsu vārṣhṇeya jāyate varṇa-saṅkaraḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.40 (1.39 to 1.42)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.41 (39-42): An increase of such an uncultured population drags the entire family and the ones who have destroyed it, to hell. Even their forefathers fall, being deprived of their offerings of food and water.
shlokaList.name: An increase of such an uncultured population drags the entire family and the ones who have destroyed it, to hell. Even their forefathers fall, being deprived of their offerings of food and water.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
saṅkaro narakāyaiva kula-ghnānāṁ kulasya cha
patanti pitaro hy eṣhāṁ lupta-piṇḍodaka-kriyāḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.41 (1.39 to 1.42)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.42 (39-42): Through the misdeeds of those who destroy families and are the cause of giving birth to uncultured progeny, the age-old systems of social and family traditions also get destroyed.
shlokaList.name: Through the misdeeds of those who destroy families and are the cause of giving birth to uncultured progeny, the age-old systems of social and family traditions also get destroyed.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
doṣhair etaiḥ kula-ghnānāṁ varṇa-saṅkara-kārakaiḥ
utsādyante jāti-dharmāḥ kula-dharmāśh cha śhāśhvatāḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.42 (1.39 to 1.42)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.43: O Janardana! I have heard from learned men that people whose family traditions are compromised permanently live in hell.
shlokaList.name: O Janardana! I have heard from learned men that people whose family traditions are compromised permanently live in hell.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
utsanna-kula-dharmāṇāṁ manuṣhyāṇāṁ janārdana
narake ‘niyataṁ vāso bhavatītyanuśhuśhruma
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.43

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.44: Alas! Isn’t it strange that we are thinking of indulging in this great sin of killing our own kinsmen for the sake of enjoying a kingdom.
shlokaList.name: Alas! Isn’t it strange that we are thinking of indulging in this great sin of killing our own kinsmen for the sake of enjoying a kingdom.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
aho bata mahat pāpaṁ kartuṁ vyavasitā vayam
yad rājya-sukha-lobhena hantuṁ sva-janam udyatāḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.44

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.45: Instead it would be better, if the sons of Dhritrashtra, equipped with weapons on the battlefield, were to kill me unarmed and defenceless.
shlokaList.name: Instead it would be better, if the sons of Dhritrashtra, equipped with weapons on the battlefield, were to kill me unarmed and defenceless.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yadi mām apratīkāram aśhastraṁ śhastra-pāṇayaḥ
dhārtarāṣhṭrā raṇe hanyus tan me kṣhemataraṁ bhavet
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.45

shlokaList.description: Shloka 1.46: Arjuna having said this collapsed onto the seat of his chariot setting aside his bow and arrows, his mind overwhelmed with grief.
shlokaList.name: Arjuna having said this collapsed onto the seat of his chariot setting aside his bow and arrows, his mind overwhelmed with grief.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
sañjaya uvāca 
evam uktvārjunaḥ saṅkhye rathopastha upāviśat 
visṛjya sa-śaraṁ cāpaṁ śoka-saṁvigna-mānasaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 1.46

chapterList.description: Chapter 2: Sankhya Yoga - The Yoga of Knowledge and  Discernment
chapterList.name: Chapter 2: Sankhya Yoga - The Yoga of Knowledge and  Discernment

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.1: Sanjaya said - Upon seeing Arjuna, who was overwhelmed with sorrow, tears streaming from his eyes as he was feeling despondent, Madhusudana spoke the following words.
shlokaList.name: Sanjaya said - Upon seeing Arjuna, who was overwhelmed with sorrow, tears streaming from his eyes as he was feeling despondent, Madhusudana spoke the following words.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
sañjaya uvācha
tam tathā kṛipayāviṣhṭamaśhru pūrṇākulekṣhaṇam
viṣhīdantamidam vākyam uvācha madhusūdanaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.1

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.2: Bhagawan Sri Krishna said - From where have these impure thoughts come to you at such a critical moment? This behavior is unbecoming of an Arya and does not lead to heaven; instead, it brings dishonour, O Arjuna!
shlokaList.name: Bhagawan Sri Krishna said - From where have these impure thoughts come to you at such a critical moment? This behavior is unbecoming of an Arya and does not lead to heaven; instead, it brings dishonour, O Arjuna!
shlokaList.sanskritText:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
kutastvā kaśhmalamidam viṣhame samupasthitam
anārya-juṣhṭamaswargyam akīrti-karam arjuna
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.2

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.3: O Partha, do not yield to this degrading incompetence. Let it not overcome you. Cast aside such petty weakness of the heart and arise, O Parantapa!
shlokaList.name: O Partha, do not yield to this degrading incompetence. Let it not overcome you. Cast aside such petty weakness of the heart and arise, O Parantapa!
shlokaList.sanskritText:
klaibyam mā sma gamaḥ pārtha naitat tvayyupapadyate
kṣhudram hṛidaya-daurbalyam tyaktvottiṣhṭha parantapa
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.3

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.4: Arjuna said - O Madhusudana! How can I battle against Bhiṣma and Droṇa in this war? They are worthy of my worship. O destroyer of the enemies! Please tell me.
shlokaList.name: Arjuna said - O Madhusudana! How can I battle against Bhiṣma and Droṇa in this war? They are worthy of my worship. O destroyer of the enemies! Please tell me.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
arjuna uvācha
katham bhīṣhmam aham sankhye droṇam cha madhusūdana
iṣhubhiḥ pratiyotsyāmi pūjārhāvari-sūdana
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.4

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.5: It is better to live in this world by begging than to live at the cost of the lives of my revered gurus. Despite their greed, they are nonetheless our superiors. If we kill these esteemed teachers, for the sake of wealth and other material desires, then our spoils would get tainted, akin to a feast tainted with blood.
shlokaList.name: It is better to live in this world by begging than to live at the cost of the lives of my revered gurus. Despite their greed, they are nonetheless our superiors. If we kill these esteemed teachers, for the sake of wealth and other material desires, then our spoils would get tainted, akin to a feast tainted with blood.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
gurūnahatvā hi mahānubhāvān
śhreyo bhoktum bhaikṣhyamapīha loke
hatvārtha-kāmāmstu gurūnihaiva
bhuñjīya bhogān rudhira-pradigdhān
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.5

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.6: Nor do I know whether it would be better if we defeat them (the Kauravas) or if they defeat us. I have no desire to live after slaying them. Yet, here stand the sons of Dhritarashtra, prepared to fight against us on this battlefield.
shlokaList.name: Nor do I know whether it would be better if we defeat them (the Kauravas) or if they defeat us. I have no desire to live after slaying them. Yet, here stand the sons of Dhritarashtra, prepared to fight against us on this battlefield.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
na chaitadvidmaḥ kataranno garīyo
yadvā jayema yadi vā no jayeyuḥ
yāneva hatvā na jijīviṣhāmas
te ’vasthitāḥ pramukhe dhārtarāṣhṭrāḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.6

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.7: My inherent virtues are tainted by mental weakness and my intellect is confused regarding my duties. I implore you, O Krishna, to definitively guide me towards what is the right thing to do. I am your disciple, surrendering myself to you, please instruct me.
shlokaList.name: My inherent virtues are tainted by mental weakness and my intellect is confused regarding my duties. I implore you, O Krishna, to definitively guide me towards what is the right thing to do. I am your disciple, surrendering myself to you, please instruct me.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
kārpaṇya-doṣhopahata-svabhāvaḥ
pṛichchhāmi tvām dharma-sammūḍha-chetāḥ
yach-chhreyaḥ syānniśhchitam brūhi tanme
śhiṣhyaste ’ham śhādhi mām tvām prapannam
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.7

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.8: I see nothing that can alleviate this sorrow which is drying up my senses, not even attaining a prosperous and unconquerable kingdom on Earth or gaining dominion like the celestial beings themselves.
shlokaList.name: I see nothing that can alleviate this sorrow which is drying up my senses, not even attaining a prosperous and unconquerable kingdom on Earth or gaining dominion like the celestial beings themselves.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
na hi prapaśhyāmi mamāpanudyād
yach-chhokam uchchhoṣhaṇam-indriyāṇām
avāpya bhūmāv-asapatnamṛiddham
rājyam surāṇāmapi chādhipatyam
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.8

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.9 (9-10): Sanjaya said - “O Govind! I will not fight,” said Arjuna, the conqueror of enemies, to Hrishikesh (Sri Krishna), and then Arjuna fell silent.
shlokaList.name: Sanjaya said - “O Govind! I will not fight,” said Arjuna, the conqueror of enemies, to Hrishikesh (Sri Krishna), and then Arjuna fell silent.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
sañjaya uvācha
evam-uktvā hṛiṣhīkeśham guḍākeśhaḥ parantapa
na yotsya iti govindam uktvā tūṣhṇīm babhūva ha
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.9 (2.9 to 2.10)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.10 (9-10): O descendent of Bharata! As Lord Krishna stood between the two armies, gently smiling, He addressed the despondent Arjuna with these words.
shlokaList.name: O descendent of Bharata! As Lord Krishna stood between the two armies, gently smiling, He addressed the despondent Arjuna with these words.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
tam-uvācha hṛiṣhīkeśhaḥ prahasanniva bhārata
senayorubhayor-madhye viṣhīdantam-idam vachaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.10 (2.9 to 2.10)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.11: Bhagawan Sri Krishna said - Even though you speak learned words, you are mourning for what is not worthy of your sorrow. Those who are wise lament neither for the living nor the dead.
shlokaList.name: Bhagawan Sri Krishna said - Even though you speak learned words, you are mourning for what is not worthy of your sorrow. Those who are wise lament neither for the living nor the dead.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
aśhochyān-anvaśhochas-tvam prajñā-vādānśh cha bhāṣhase
gatāsūn-agatāsūnśh-cha nānuśhochanti paṇḍitāḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.11

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.12: There was never a time when I, you and all these kings did not exist in the past. Similarly, there will never be a time in the future when we will cease to exist.
shlokaList.name: There was never a time when I, you and all these kings did not exist in the past. Similarly, there will never be a time in the future when we will cease to exist.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
na tvevāham jātu nāsam na tvam neme janādhipāḥ
na chaiva na bhaviṣhyāmaḥ sarve vayamataḥ param
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.12

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.13: Just as the embodied soul passes through various stages of life within the body—childhood, youth and old age—similarly, the soul transmigrates to another body after death. Understanding this truth, the self-realised soul does not become bewildered by such a change.
shlokaList.name: Just as the embodied soul passes through various stages of life within the body—childhood, youth and old age—similarly, the soul transmigrates to another body after death. Understanding this truth, the self-realised soul does not become bewildered by such a change.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
dehino ’smin yathā dehe kaumāram yauvanam jarā
tathā dehāntara-prāptir dhīras tatra na muhyati
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.13

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.14: O Kaunteya! The fleeting nature of happiness and distress, which come and go like the changing winter and summer seasons, are rooted in sense perception. One must learn to tolerate them, without being disturbed, O scion of Bharata!
shlokaList.name: O Kaunteya! The fleeting nature of happiness and distress, which come and go like the changing winter and summer seasons, are rooted in sense perception. One must learn to tolerate them, without being disturbed, O scion of Bharata!
shlokaList.sanskritText:
mātrā-sparśhās tu kaunteya śhītoṣhṇa-sukha-duḥkha-dāḥ
āgamāpāyino ’nityās tāns-titikṣhasva bhārata
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.14

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.15: O best among humans! One who remains unaffected by these fluctuations, maintaining equanimity in sorrow and happiness, becomes qualified for immortality.
shlokaList.name: O best among humans! One who remains unaffected by these fluctuations, maintaining equanimity in sorrow and happiness, becomes qualified for immortality.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yam hi na vyathayantyete puruṣham puruṣharṣhabha
sama-duḥkha-sukham dhīram so ’mṛitatvāya kalpate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.15

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.16: The unreal has no true existence and the real is eternal and cannot be non-existent. This understanding of the nature of these two has been realized by the wise seers of truth.
shlokaList.name: The unreal has no true existence and the real is eternal and cannot be non-existent. This understanding of the nature of these two has been realized by the wise seers of truth.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
nāsato vidyate bhāvo nābhāvo vidyate sataḥ
ubhayorapi dṛiṣhṭo ’nta stvanayos tattva-darśhibhiḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.16

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.17 (17-18): You should know that which pervades the entire body is imperishable. Nothing can ever cause the destruction of this eternal being.
shlokaList.name: You should know that which pervades the entire body is imperishable. Nothing can ever cause the destruction of this eternal being.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
avināśhi tu tadviddhi yena sarvam idam tatam
vināśham avyayasyāsya na kaśhchit kartum arhati
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.17 (2.17 to 2.18)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.18 (17-18): The soul dwelling within the body is eternal, indestructible and incomprehensible. However, these physical bodies have an end. Therefore, O scion of Bharata, you should engage in the battle!
shlokaList.name: The soul dwelling within the body is eternal, indestructible and incomprehensible. However, these physical bodies have an end. Therefore, O scion of Bharata, you should engage in the battle!
shlokaList.sanskritText:
antavanta ime dehā nityasyoktāḥ śharīriṇaḥ
anāśhino ’prameyasya tasmād yudhyasva bhārata
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.18 (2.17 to 2.18)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.19: Those who believe the soul can kill or be killed are equally unaware of the truth, because the soul neither kills nor is killed.
shlokaList.name: Those who believe the soul can kill or be killed are equally unaware of the truth, because the soul neither kills nor is killed.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
ya enam vetti hantāram yaśh chainam manyate hatam
ubhau tau na vijānīto nāyam hanti na hanyate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.19

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.20: The soul is never born, nor does it ever die; nor does it come into being with the creation of the body and neither does it ever cease to exist. It is birthless, eternal, immortal and timeless. The soul cannot be destroyed, even if the body is slain.
shlokaList.name: The soul is never born, nor does it ever die; nor does it come into being with the creation of the body and neither does it ever cease to exist. It is birthless, eternal, immortal and timeless. The soul cannot be destroyed, even if the body is slain.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
na jāyate mriyate vā kadāchin
nāyam bhūtvā bhavitā vā na bhūyaḥ
ajo nityaḥ śhāśhvato ’yam purāṇo
na hanyate hanyamāne śharīre
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.20

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.21: O Partha! One who understands the soul as eternal, unborn, undecaying and indestructible—how can such a person cause death to anyone or instigate anyone else to kill?
shlokaList.name: O Partha! One who understands the soul as eternal, unborn, undecaying and indestructible—how can such a person cause death to anyone or instigate anyone else to kill?
shlokaList.sanskritText:
vedāvināśhinam nityam ya enam ajam avyayam
katham sa puruṣhaḥ pārtha kam ghātayati hanti kam
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.21

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.22: Just as people discard old clothes after using them and put on new ones, similarly, the soul leaves the old, ailing body and enters into a new body.
shlokaList.name: Just as people discard old clothes after using them and put on new ones, similarly, the soul leaves the old, ailing body and enters into a new body.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
vāsānsi jīrṇāni yathā vihāya
navāni gṛihṇāti naro ’parāṇi
tathā śharīrāṇi vihāya jīrṇānya
nyāni sanyāti navāni dehī
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.22

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.23 (23-25): The soul cannot be cut by a sword, burnt by fire. It cannot get wet with water; it cannot be dried up by the air.
shlokaList.name: The soul cannot be cut by a sword, burnt by fire. It cannot get wet with water; it cannot be dried up by the air.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
nainam chhindanti śhastrāṇi nainam dahati pāvakaḥ
na chainam kledayantyāpo na śhoṣhayati mārutaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.23 (2.23 to 2.25)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.24 (23-25): The soul cannot be severed, is not combustible, does not dissolve and does not dry up. It is eternal, present everywhere, unchanging, permanent and everlasting.
shlokaList.name: The soul cannot be severed, is not combustible, does not dissolve and does not dry up. It is eternal, present everywhere, unchanging, permanent and everlasting.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
achchhedyo ’yam adāhyo ’yam akledyo ’śhoṣhya eva cha
nityaḥ sarva-gataḥ sthāṇur achalo ’yam sanātanaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.24 (2.23 to 2.25)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.25 (23-25): The soul is unmanifest, inconceivable and unchangeable. Therefore, having understood the real nature of the soul, you should not be sorrowful.
shlokaList.name: The soul is unmanifest, inconceivable and unchangeable. Therefore, having understood the real nature of the soul, you should not be sorrowful.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
avyakto ’yam achintyo ’yam avikāryo ’yam uchyate
tasmādevam viditvainam nānuśhochitum arhasi
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.25 (2.23 to 2.25)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.26: O mighty armed Arjuna! Even if you perceive the soul as subject to the eternal cycle of birth and death, still you should not succumb to despair.
shlokaList.name: O mighty armed Arjuna! Even if you perceive the soul as subject to the eternal cycle of birth and death, still you should not succumb to despair.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
atha chainam nitya-jātam nityam vā manyase mṛitam
tathāpi tvam mahā-bāho naivam śhochitum arhasi
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.26

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.27 (27-28): For that which is born, death is certain and for that which dies, rebirth is certain. This is an unavoidable reality. Therefore, there is no need to mourn over it.
shlokaList.name: For that which is born, death is certain and for that which dies, rebirth is certain. This is an unavoidable reality. Therefore, there is no need to mourn over it.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
jātasya hi dhruvo mṛityur dhruvam janma mṛitasya cha
tasmād aparihārye ’rthe na tvam śhochitum arhasi
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.27 (2.27 to 2.28)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.28 (27-28): Before birth, everyone was unmanifest and after death, everyone will become unmanifest again. The manifested state in between is transient. Therefore, what is there to lament?
shlokaList.name: Before birth, everyone was unmanifest and after death, everyone will become unmanifest again. The manifested state in between is transient. Therefore, what is there to lament?
shlokaList.sanskritText:
avyaktādīni bhūtāni vyakta-madhyāni bhārata
avyakta-nidhanānyeva tatra kā paridevanā
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.28 (2.27 to 2.28)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.29: Some perceive it as a marvel, others speak of it as extraordinary and many hear of it with wonder. Yet others, even after hearing about it, cannot understand it at all.
shlokaList.name: Some perceive it as a marvel, others speak of it as extraordinary and many hear of it with wonder. Yet others, even after hearing about it, cannot understand it at all.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
āśhcharya-vat paśhyati kaśhchid enan
āśhcharya-vad vadati tathaiva chānyaḥ
āśhcharya-vach chainam anyaḥ śhṛiṇoti
śhrutvāpyenam veda na chaiva kaśhchit
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.29

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.30: O scion of Bharata! The soul that resides within every body is eternal and cannot be destroyed. Therefore, you should not grieve for anyone.
shlokaList.name: O scion of Bharata! The soul that resides within every body is eternal and cannot be destroyed. Therefore, you should not grieve for anyone.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
dehī nityam avadhyo ’yam dehe sarvasya bhārata
tasmāt sarvāṇi bhūtāni na tvam śhochitum arhasi
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.30

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.31: Considering your social duties as a warrior, you should not be hesitant to fight. For a Kṣatriya, there is nothing more honourable than engaging in a righteous war.
shlokaList.name: Considering your social duties as a warrior, you should not be hesitant to fight. For a Kṣatriya, there is nothing more honourable than engaging in a righteous war.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
swa-dharmam api chāvekṣhya na vikampitum arhasi
dharmyāddhi yuddhāch chhreyo ’nyat kṣhatriyasya na vidyate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.31

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.32: The gateway to heaven has been opened before you effortlessly. O Partha! Fortunate are those Kshatriyas who receive such an opportunity to engage in a righteous battle.
shlokaList.name: The gateway to heaven has been opened before you effortlessly. O Partha! Fortunate are those Kshatriyas who receive such an opportunity to engage in a righteous battle.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yadṛichchhayā chopapannam swarga-dvāram apāvṛitam
sukhinaḥ kṣhatriyāḥ pārtha labhante yuddham īdṛiśham
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.32

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.33: Therefore, if you do not engage in this righteous battle, you will incur sin for dishonouring your duty as a warrior and tarnish your glory too.
shlokaList.name: Therefore, if you do not engage in this righteous battle, you will incur sin for dishonouring your duty as a warrior and tarnish your glory too.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
atha chet tvam imam dharmyam saṅgrāmam na kariṣhyasi
tataḥ sva-dharmam kīrtim cha hitvā pāpam avāpsyasi
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.33

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.34 (34-36): People will forever speak disgracefully of you. For one who has always been honoured, dishonour is more grievous than death.
shlokaList.name: People will forever speak disgracefully of you. For one who has always been honoured, dishonour is more grievous than death.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
akīrtim chāpi bhūtāni kathayiṣhyanti te ’vyayām
sambhāvitasya chākīrtir maraṇād atirichyate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.34 (2.34 to 2.36)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.35 (34-36): Other great warriors will perceive that you fled from the battlefield out of fear. Those who held you in high regard will think less of you.
shlokaList.name: Other great warriors will perceive that you fled from the battlefield out of fear. Those who held you in high regard will think less of you.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
bhayād raṇād uparatam mansyante tvām mahā-rathāḥ
yeṣhām cha tvam bahu-mato bhūtvā yāsyasi lāghavam
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.35 (2.34 to 2.36)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.36 (34-36): Your enemies will taunt you with disparaging words, questioning your courage and ability. What could be more painful than that?
shlokaList.name: Your enemies will taunt you with disparaging words, questioning your courage and ability. What could be more painful than that?
shlokaList.sanskritText:
avāchya-vādānśh cha bahūn vadiṣhyanti tavāhitāḥ
nindantastava sāmarthyam tato duḥkhataram nu kim
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.36 (2.34 to 2.36)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.37: : If you die in this righteous war, you will attain heaven. If you emerge victorious, you will rule over the world. Therefore, arise, Arjuna, be determined and prepare yourself for battle.
shlokaList.name: If you die in this righteous war, you will attain heaven. If you emerge victorious, you will rule over the world. Therefore, arise, Arjuna, be determined and prepare yourself for battle.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
hato vā prāpsyasi swargam jitvā vā bhokṣhyase mahīm
tasmād uttiṣhṭha kaunteya yuddhāya kṛita-niśhchayaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.37

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.38: Fight as a matter of duty, regarding equally happiness and distress, victory and defeat, profit and loss. In this way, you can act without incurring sin.
shlokaList.name: Fight as a matter of duty, regarding equally happiness and distress, victory and defeat, profit and loss. In this way, you can act without incurring sin.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
sukha-duḥkhe same kṛitvā lābhālābhau jayājayau
tato yuddhāya yujyasva naivam pāpam avāpsyasi
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.38

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.39: O Partha! I have thus described knowledge of Sankhya Yoga. Now, listen to it from the perspective of Buddhi Yoga. When you work with this wisdom, it will liberate you from the bondage of actions.
shlokaList.name: O Partha! I have thus described knowledge of Sankhya Yoga. Now, listen to it from the perspective of Buddhi Yoga. When you work with this wisdom, it will liberate you from the bondage of actions.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
eṣhā te ’bhihitā sānkhye buddhir yoge tvimām śhṛiṇu
buddhyā yukto yayā pārtha karma-bandham prahāsyasi
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.39

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.40: In this practice, no effort is ever wasted and there is no adverse outcome. Even a small amount of practice on this path can alleviate one from great fear and uncertainty.
shlokaList.name: In this practice, no effort is ever wasted and there is no adverse outcome. Even a small amount of practice on this path can alleviate one from great fear and uncertainty.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
nehābhikrama-nāśho ’sti pratyavāyo na vidyate
svalpam apyasya dharmasya trāyate mahato bhayāt
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.40

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.41: O son of the Kurus! The intelligence of those on this path is one-pointed and focused on one goal, whereas the intelligence of those who are not determined is scattered across many branches.
shlokaList.name: O son of the Kurus! The intelligence of those on this path is one-pointed and focused on one goal, whereas the intelligence of those who are not determined is scattered across many branches.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
vyavasāyātmikā buddhir ekeha kuru-nandana
bahu-śhākhā hyanantāśh cha buddhayo ’vyavasāyinām
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.41

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.42 (42-43): Those who speak enticingly about the fruits of actions are people of limited understanding. They are devoted superficially to the words of the Vedas, asserting, “There is nothing beyond this”;
shlokaList.name: Those who speak enticingly about the fruits of actions are people of limited understanding. They are devoted superficially to the words of the Vedas, asserting, “There is nothing beyond this”;
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yāmimām puṣhpitām vācham pravadanty-avipaśhchitaḥ
veda-vāda-ratāḥ pārtha nānyad astīti vādinaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.42 (2.42 to 2.43)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.43 (42-43): They are driven by desires and view heavenly rewards as the ultimate goal. They advocate numerous elaborate rituals described in the Vedas that promise worldly pleasures, power and great birth.
shlokaList.name: They are driven by desires and view heavenly rewards as the ultimate goal. They advocate numerous elaborate rituals described in the Vedas that promise worldly pleasures, power and great birth.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
kāmātmānaḥ swarga-parā janma-karma-phala-pradām
kriyā-viśheṣha-bahulām bhogaiśhwarya-gatim prati
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.43 (2.42 to 2.43)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.44: Those addicted to such pleasures and power have their minds ensnared by them. Such individuals cannot have the fixed intelligence determined to attain the state of Samadhi.
shlokaList.name: Those addicted to such pleasures and power have their minds ensnared by them. Such individuals cannot have the fixed intelligence determined to attain the state of Samadhi.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
bhogaiśwvarya-prasaktānām tayāpahṛita-chetasām
vyavasāyātmikā buddhiḥ samādhau na vidhīyate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.44

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.45: The Vedas primarily address matters related to the three Guṇas, O Arjuna! Strive to transcend attachment to these three Guṇas. By doing so, you can overcome all dualities, be situated in a state of sattva, remain detached from concerns of gain and protection and attain self-mastery.
shlokaList.name: The Vedas primarily address matters related to the three Guṇas, O Arjuna! Strive to transcend attachment to these three Guṇas. By doing so, you can overcome all dualities, be situated in a state of sattva, remain detached from concerns of gain and protection and attain self-mastery.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
trai-guṇya-viṣhayā vedā nistrai-guṇyo bhavārjuna
nirdvandvo nitya-sattva-stho niryoga-kṣhema ātmavān
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.45

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.46: Just as a person standing before a vast, overflowing lake does not have to concern himself with a small water source, so too, the wise who attain knowledge of the Supreme Brahman does not have to concern themselves with the fruitive knowledge contained in the Vedas.
shlokaList.name: Just as a person standing before a vast, overflowing lake does not have to concern himself with a small water source, so too, the wise who attain knowledge of the Supreme Brahman does not have to concern themselves with the fruitive knowledge contained in the Vedas.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yāvān artha udapāne sarvataḥ samplutodake
tāvānsarveṣhu vedeṣhu brāhmaṇasya vijānataḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.46

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.47: You have the right to perform your actions, but you have no rights over the results of those actions. Do not attribute the outcome solely to yourself, nor be attached to not performing your duties.
shlokaList.name: You have the right to perform your actions, but you have no rights over the results of those actions. Do not attribute the outcome solely to yourself, nor be attached to not performing your duties.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
karmaṇy-evādhikāras te mā phaleṣhu kadāchana
mā karma-phala-hetur bhūr mā te saṅgo ’stvakarmaṇi
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.47

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.48: O Dhananjaya! Perform your duties with a steady mind, remaining equipoised and free from attachment from success or failure. This equanimity is known as Yoga.
shlokaList.name: O Dhananjaya! Perform your duties with a steady mind, remaining equipoised and free from attachment from success or failure. This equanimity is known as Yoga.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yoga-sthaḥ kuru karmāṇi saṅgam tyaktvā dhanañjaya
siddhy-asiddhyoḥ samo bhūtvā samatvam yoga uchyate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.48

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.49: O Dhananjaya! Taking refuge in buddhi or intelligence, perform actions without any desire for the results, keeping away actions performed with desire, which are inferior. Pitiable are those who act solely for the sake of results.
shlokaList.name: O Dhananjaya! Taking refuge in buddhi or intelligence, perform actions without any desire for the results, keeping away actions performed with desire, which are inferior. Pitiable are those who act solely for the sake of results.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
dūreṇa hy-avaram karma buddhi-yogād dhanañjaya
buddhau śharaṇam anvichchha kṛipaṇāḥ phala-hetavaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.49

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.50: Equipped with deliberate intelligence, one who works without attachment to the results, can transcend the effects of both good and bad actions in this birth itself. Therefore, you should cultivate this Yoga, which is the art of skillful work.
shlokaList.name: Equipped with deliberate intelligence, one who works without attachment to the results, can transcend the effects of both good and bad actions in this birth itself. Therefore, you should cultivate this Yoga, which is the art of skillful work.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
buddhi-yukto jahātīha ubhe sukṛita-duṣhkṛite
tasmād yogāya yujyasva yogaḥ karmasu kauśhalam
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.50

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.51: Wise men of stable intelligence, renounce attachment to the fruits of their actions and thus liberate themselves from the cycle of birth and death and attain the supreme state free of any misery.
shlokaList.name: Wise men of stable intelligence, renounce attachment to the fruits of their actions and thus liberate themselves from the cycle of birth and death and attain the supreme state free of any misery.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
karma-jam buddhi-yuktā hi phalam tyaktvā manīṣhiṇaḥ
janma-bandha-vinirmuktāḥ padam gachchhanty-anāmayam
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.51

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.52: When your intelligence has crossed over the thick forest of delusion, you will develop indifference towards what has been heard and what is yet to be heard.
shlokaList.name: When your intelligence has crossed over the thick forest of delusion, you will develop indifference towards what has been heard and what is yet to be heard.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yadā te moha-kalilam buddhir vyatitariṣhyati
tadā gantāsi nirvedam śhrotavyasya śhrutasya cha
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.52

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.53: When your intellect is no more attracted to the fruitive sections of the Vedas and when it remains steadfast in a state of samadhi, then it attains the state of Yoga.
shlokaList.name: When your intellect is no more attracted to the fruitive sections of the Vedas and when it remains steadfast in a state of samadhi, then it attains the state of Yoga.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
śhruti-vipratipannā te yadā sthāsyati niśhchalā
samādhāv-achalā buddhis tadā yogam avāpsyasi
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.53

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.54: Arjuna said - O Keshava! What are the characteristics of a person who has attained the stage of steady intelligence and is situated in samadhi? How does such a person speak, how does he sit and how does he walk?
shlokaList.name: Arjuna said - O Keshava! What are the characteristics of a person who has attained the stage of steady intelligence and is situated in samadhi? How does such a person speak, how does he sit and how does he walk?
shlokaList.sanskritText:
arjuna uvācha
sthita-prajñasya kā bhāṣhā samādhi-sthasya keśhava
sthita-dhīḥ kim prabhāṣheta kim āsīta vrajeta kim
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.54

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.55: Bhagawan Sri Krishna said - O Arjuna! When one completely renounces all desires born of the mind’s wanderings and finds contentment within oneself through self-knowledge, such a person is known as one with steady intelligence.
shlokaList.name: Bhagawan Sri Krishna said - O Arjuna! When one completely renounces all desires born of the mind’s wanderings and finds contentment within oneself through self-knowledge, such a person is known as one with steady intelligence.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
prajahāti yadā kāmān sarvān pārtha mano-gatān
ātmany-evātmanā tuṣhṭaḥ sthita-prajñas tadochyate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.55

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.56: The person whose mind remains unruffled in the face of adversity, who does not get carried away by happiness and who has conquered desire, fear and anger is called a sage of steady intelligence.
shlokaList.name: The person whose mind remains unruffled in the face of adversity, who does not get carried away by happiness and who has conquered desire, fear and anger is called a sage of steady intelligence.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
duḥkheṣhv-anudvigna-manāḥ sukheṣhu vigata-spṛihaḥ
vīta-rāga-bhaya-krodhaḥ sthita-dhīr munir uchyate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.56

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.57: The one who remains unattached to whatever situations arise, whether favourable or unfavourable, neither harbouring likes nor dislikes is firmly established in perfect wisdom.
shlokaList.name: The one who remains unattached to whatever situations arise, whether favourable or unfavourable, neither harbouring likes nor dislikes is firmly established in perfect wisdom.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yaḥ sarvatrānabhisnehas tat tat prāpya śhubhāśhubham
nābhinandati na dveṣhṭi tasya prajñā pratiṣhṭhitā
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.57

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.58: When a person withdraws their senses from external distractions, like a tortoise withdraws its limbs into its shell, he is to be considered firmly established in perfect wisdom.
shlokaList.name: When a person withdraws their senses from external distractions, like a tortoise withdraws its limbs into its shell, he is to be considered firmly established in perfect wisdom.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yadā sanharate chāyam kūrmo ’ṅgānīva sarvaśhaḥ
indriyāṇīndriyārthebhyas tasya prajñā pratiṣhṭhitā
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.58

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.59: A person may stop indulging in sensory objects, but the craving for those sense objects may still persist. However, this craving also disappears when one experiences something higher.
shlokaList.name: A person may stop indulging in sensory objects, but the craving for those sense objects may still persist. However, this craving also disappears when one experiences something higher.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
viṣhayā vinivartante nirāhārasya dehinaḥ
rasa-varjam raso ’pyasya param dṛiṣhṭvā nivartate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.59

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.60: O Kaunteya! These restless senses can forcibly distract even the mind of a highly wise person striving for perfection.
shlokaList.name: O Kaunteya! These restless senses can forcibly distract even the mind of a highly wise person striving for perfection.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yatato hyapi kaunteya puruṣhasya vipaśhchitaḥ
indriyāṇi pramāthīni haranti prasabham manaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.60

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.61: Therefore, by controlling all the senses and practicing yoga, one should fix his consciousness upon Me. For one who has mastered control over the senses, he is to be considered firmly established in perfect wisdom.
shlokaList.name: Therefore, by controlling all the senses and practicing yoga, one should fix his consciousness upon Me . For one who has mastered control over the senses, he is to be considered firmly established in perfect wisdom.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
tāni sarvāṇi sanyamya yukta āsīta mat-paraḥ
vaśhe hi yasyendriyāṇi tasya prajñā pratiṣhṭhitā
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.61

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.62 (62-63): When a person constantly dwells on sense objects, attachment to those objects develops. This attachment leads to desire and from desire arises anger.
shlokaList.name: When a person constantly dwells on sense objects, attachment to those objects develops. This attachment leads to desire and from desire arises anger.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
dhyāyato viṣhayān pumsaḥ saṅgas teṣhūpajāyate
saṅgāt sañjāyate kāmaḥ kāmāt krodho ’bhijāyate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.62 (2.62 to 2.63)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.63 (62-63): From anger arises complete delusion and from such delusion comes loss of memory. When memory is disturbed, intelligence is destroyed and this loss of intelligence leads to the destruction of the individual.
shlokaList.name: From anger arises complete delusion and from such delusion comes loss of memory. When memory is disturbed, intelligence is destroyed and this loss of intelligence leads to the destruction of the individual.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
krodhād bhavati sammohaḥ sammohāt smṛiti-vibhramaḥ
smṛiti-bhranśhād buddhi-nāśho buddhi-nāśhāt praṇaśhyati
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.63 (2.62 to 2.63)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.64: But one whose mind is completely disciplined and senses under control, one who harbours neither likes nor dislikes, even while interacting with sense objects, can attain the grace of the Supreme.
shlokaList.name: But one whose mind is completely disciplined and senses under control, one who harbours neither likes nor dislikes, even while interacting with sense objects, can attain the grace of the Supreme.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
rāga-dveṣha-viyuktais tu viṣhayān indriyaiśh charan
ātma-vaśhyair-vidheyātmā prasādam adhigachchhati
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.64

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.65: Upon achieving such grace, all sorrows end. And in a happy state of mind, one’s intellect becomes completely fixed.
shlokaList.name: Upon achieving such grace, all sorrows end. And in a happy state of mind, one’s intellect becomes completely fixed.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
prasāde sarva-duḥkhānām hānir asyopajāyate
prasanna-chetaso hyāśhu buddhiḥ paryavatiṣhṭhate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.65

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.66: Conversely, one who is not connected with the Supreme, cannot have stable intelligence or a fixed mind. Without them there cannot be any peace. And for one who lacks peace, there is no question of happiness too.
shlokaList.name: Conversely, one who is not connected with the Supreme, cannot have stable intelligence or a fixed mind. Without them there cannot be any peace. And for one who lacks peace, there is no question of happiness too.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
nāsti buddhir-ayuktasya na chāyuktasya bhāvanā
na chābhāvayataḥ śhāntir aśhāntasya kutaḥ sukham
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.66

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.67: The mind that constantly follows any of the wandering senses, loses its intelligence, just as the wind sweeps away a boat on water away from its course.
shlokaList.name: The mind that constantly follows any of the wandering senses, loses its intelligence, just as the wind sweeps away a boat on water away from its course.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
indriyāṇām hi charatām yan mano ’nuvidhīyate
tadasya harati prajñām vāyur nāvam ivāmbhasi
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.67

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.68: Therefore, O mighty-armed Arjuna, one whose senses are completely withdrawn from their objects, he is to be considered firmly established in perfect wisdom.
shlokaList.name: Therefore, O mighty-armed Arjuna, one whose senses are completely withdrawn from their objects, he is to be considered firmly established in perfect wisdom.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
tasmād yasya mahā-bāho nigṛihītāni sarvaśhaḥ
indriyāṇīndriyārthebhyas tasya prajñā pratiṣhṭhitā
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.68

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.69: What is night for other beings, is daylight for the self-controlled. And what is daylight for other beings, is perceived as night by the wise.
shlokaList.name: What is night for other beings, is daylight for the self-controlled. And what is daylight for other beings, is perceived as night by the wise.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yā niśhā sarva-bhūtānām tasyām jāgarti sanyamī
yasyām jāgrati bhūtāni sā niśhā paśhyato muneḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.69

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.70: Just as the ocean is constantly being filled by the rivers but remains undisturbed, similarly, one who is not disturbed by constant flow of desires, actually attains peace and not one who attempts to fulfill them.
shlokaList.name: Just as the ocean is constantly being filled by the rivers but remains undisturbed, similarly, one who is not disturbed by constant flow of desires, actually attains peace and not one who attempts to fulfill them.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
āpūryamāṇam achala-pratiṣhṭham
samudram āpaḥ praviśhanti yadvat
tadvat kāmā yam praviśhanti sarve
sa śhāntim āpnoti na kāma-kāmī
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.70

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.71: The one who has renounced all desires and lives freely without desires, devoid of egoism and possessiveness, can actually attain peace.
shlokaList.name: The one who has renounced all desires and lives freely without desires, devoid of egoism and possessiveness, can actually attain peace.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
vihāya kāmān yaḥ sarvān pumānśh charati niḥspṛihaḥ
nirmamo nirahankāraḥ sa śhāntim adhigachchhati
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.71

shlokaList.description: Shloka 2.72: O Arjuna! This is the spiritual state, having attained which one is never illusioned. Remaining steadfast in this state, even at the time of death, one attains nirvaṇa, liberation from the cycle of birth and death and enters into the supreme abode.
shlokaList.name: O Arjuna! This is the spiritual state, having attained which one is never illusioned. Remaining steadfast in this state, even at the time of death, one attains nirvaṇa, liberation from the cycle of birth and death and enters into the supreme abode.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
eṣhā brāhmī sthitiḥ pārtha nainām prāpya vimuhyati
sthitvāsyām anta-kāle ’pi brahma-nirvāṇam ṛichchhati
shlokaList.title: Shloka 2.72

chapterList.description: Chapter 3: Karma Yoga - The Yoga of Righteous Action
chapterList.name: Chapter 3: Karma Yoga - The Yoga of Righteous Action

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.1 (1-2): Arjuna said - O Janardana! If you consider knowledge superior to action,then O Keshava, why are you urging me to engage in this dreadful task?
shlokaList.name: Arjuna said - O Janardana! If you consider knowledge superior to action,then O Keshava, why are you urging me to engage in this dreadful task?
shlokaList.sanskritText:
arjuna uvācha
jyāyasī chet karmaṇas te matā buddhir janārdana
tat kim karmaṇi ghore mām niyojayasi keśhava
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.1 (3.1 to 3.2)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.2 (1-2): Your ambiguous instructions have bewildered my intelligence. I request you to please guide me clearly with a definitive answer as to what is most beneficial for me.
shlokaList.name: Your ambiguous instructions have bewildered my intelligence. I request you to please guide me clearly with a definitive answer as to what is most beneficial for me.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
vyāmiśhreṇeva vākyena buddhim mohayasīva me
tad ekam vada niśhchitya yena śhreyo ’ham āpnuyām
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.2 (3.1 to 3.2)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.3: Bhagawan Sri Krishna said - O sinless one, I have previously explained two paths for spiritual achievement in this world: Gyana Yoga for those preferring the path of philosophical contemplation and Karma Yoga for those preferring the path of action.
shlokaList.name: Bhagawan Sri Krishna said - O sinless one, I have previously explained two paths for spiritual achievement in this world : Gyana Yoga for those preferring the path of philosophical contemplation and Karma Yoga for those preferring the path of action.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
loke ’smin dvi-vidhā niṣhṭhā purā proktā mayānagha
jñāna-yogena sāṅkhyānām karma-yogena yoginām
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.3

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.4: A man cannot attain freedom from reactions simply by abstaining from action, nor can one achieve spiritual perfection solely through renunciation.
shlokaList.name: A man cannot attain freedom from reactions simply by abstaining from action, nor can one achieve spiritual perfection solely through renunciation.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
na karmaṇām anārambhān naiṣhkarmyam puruṣho ’śhnute
na cha sannyasanād eva siddhim samadhigachchhati
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.4

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.5: No individual can remain without performing actions even for a single moment, as every being is inevitably driven to action by the qualities (gunas) one has received from nature (prakrti).
shlokaList.name: No individual can remain without performing actions even for a single moment, as every being is inevitably driven to action by the qualities (gunas) one has received from nature (prakrti).
shlokaList.sanskritText:
na hi kaśhchit kṣhaṇam api jātu tiṣhṭhatyakarma-kṛit
kāryate hyavaśhaḥ karma sarvaḥ prakṛiti-jair guṇaiḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.5

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.6: One who, while trying to control the sense organs continuously dwells on sense objects in the mind, is to be considered foolish and a hypocrite.
shlokaList.name: One who, while trying to control the sense organs continuously dwells on sense objects in the mind, is to be considered foolish and a hypocrite.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
karmendriyāṇi sanyamya ya āste manasā smaran
indriyārthān vimūḍhātmā mithyāchāraḥ sa uchyate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.6

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.7: Instead one who controls the senses through the mind, O Arjuna, and begins practicing Karma Yoga without any attachment, he is far better.
shlokaList.name: Instead one who controls the senses through the mind, O Arjuna, and begins practicing Karma Yoga without any attachment, he is far better.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yas tvindriyāṇi manasā niyamyārabhate ’rjuna
karmendriyaiḥ karma-yogam asaktaḥ sa viśhiṣhyate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.7

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.8: You should perform your designated duties, because action is superior to inaction. Even the basic needs of the human body cannot be fulfilled without action.
shlokaList.name: You should perform your designated duties, because action is superior to inaction. Even the basic needs of the human body cannot be fulfilled without action.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
niyatam kuru karma tvam karma jyāyo hyakarmaṇaḥ
śharīra-yātrāpi cha te na prasiddhyed akarmaṇaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.8

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.9: O Kaunteya! Work binds one in this world except for the actions performed as sacrifice for the Supreme Being. Therefore without attachment to the results, you should carry out your duties as a form of sacrifice for His satisfaction.
shlokaList.name: O Kaunteya! Work binds one in this world except for the actions performed as sacrifice for the Supreme Being. Therefore without attachment to the results, you should carry out your duties as a form of sacrifice for His satisfaction.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yajñārthāt karmaṇo ’nyatra loko ’yam karma-bandhanaḥ
tad-artham karma kaunteya mukta-saṅgaḥ samāchara
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.9

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.10 (10-11): In the beginning, Prajapati created people along with the foundation of sacrifices and instructed them, “Prosper through the performance of sacrifice; may this sacrifice fulfil all your desires.”
shlokaList.name: In the beginning, Prajapati created people along with the foundation of sacrifices and instructed them, “Prosper through the performance of sacrifice; may this sacrifice fulfil all your desires.”
shlokaList.sanskritText:
saha-yajñāḥ prajāḥ sṛiṣhṭvā purovācha prajāpatiḥ
anena prasaviṣhyadhvam eṣha vo ’stviṣhṭa-kāma-dhuk
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.10 (3.10 to 3.11)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.11 (10-11): You shall satisfy the celestial beings through sacrifice and they will satisfy you by fulfilling your desires. By mutually nourishing each other through sacrifice, supreme good will come to all.
shlokaList.name: You shall satisfy the celestial beings through sacrifice and they will satisfy you by fulfilling your desires. By mutually nourishing each other through sacrifice, supreme good will come to all.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
devān bhāvayatānena te devā bhāvayantu vaḥ
parasparam bhāvayantaḥ śhreyaḥ param avāpsyatha
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.11 (3.10 to 3.11)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.12: Pleased by the sacrifices, the celestial beings will grant you the desired rewards. However, one who enjoys these gifts from them without offering anything in return is certainly a thief.
shlokaList.name: Pleased by the sacrifices, the celestial beings will grant you the desired rewards. However, one who enjoys these gifts from them without offering anything in return is certainly a thief.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
iṣhṭān bhogān hi vo devā dāsyante yajña-bhāvitāḥ
tair dattān apradāyaibhyo yo bhuṅkte stena eva saḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.12

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.13 (13-15): The spiritually inclined person who consumes the remnants of food first offered in sacrifice, is freed from all sins. On the contrary, those who prepare food only for their own consumption eat sin itself.
shlokaList.name: The spiritually inclined person who consumes the remnants of food first offered in sacrifice, is freed from all sins. On the contrary, those who prepare food only for their own consumption eat sin itself.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yajña-śhiṣhṭāśhinaḥ santo muchyante sarva-kilbiṣhaiḥ
bhuñjate te tvagham pāpā ye pachantyātma-kāraṇāt
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.13 (3.13 to 3.15)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.14 (13-15): All living beings depend on grains or food which are produced from rains, rains are produced as a result of performing sacrifices and sacrifices are born of performance of prescribed action.
shlokaList.name: All living beings depend on grains or food which are produced from rains, rains are produced as a result of performing sacrifices and sacrifices are born of performance of prescribed action.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
annād bhavanti bhūtāni parjanyād anna-sambhavaḥ
yajñād bhavati parjanyo yajñaḥ karma-samudbhavaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.14 (3.13 to 3.15)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.15 (13-15): Understand that actions (karma) are prescribed by the Vedas, which originate directly from the eternal being (the Supreme Brahman). Therefore the all-pervading Brahman is perpetually present in the acts of sacrifice.
shlokaList.name: Understand that actions (karma) are prescribed by the Vedas, which originate directly from the eternal being (the Supreme Brahman). Therefore the all-pervading Brahman is perpetually present in the acts of sacrifice.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
karma brahmodbhavam viddhi brahmākṣhara-samudbhavam
tasmāt sarva-gatam brahma nityam yajñe pratiṣhṭhitam
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.15 (3.13 to 3.15)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.16: O Partha! Those who do not engage in this cycle of duty and sacrifice given in the Vedas live a sinful life, merely indulging in sensory pleasures and thus wasting away their lives.
shlokaList.name: O Partha! Those who do not engage in this cycle of duty and sacrifice given in the Vedas live a sinful life, merely indulging in sensory pleasures and thus wasting away their lives.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
evam pravartitam chakram nānuvartayatīha yaḥ
aghāyur indriyārāmo mogham pārtha sa jīvati
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.16

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.17 (17-18): However, the person who finds delight in the self alone, illumined from within and satisfied within themselves, for them there is no need for external actions or duties.
shlokaList.name: However, the person who finds delight in the self alone, illumined from within and satisfied within themselves, for them there is no need for external actions or duties.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yas tvātma-ratir eva syād ātma-tṛiptaśh cha mānavaḥ
ātmanyeva cha santuṣhṭas tasya kāryam na vidyate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.17 (3.17 to 3.18)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.18 (17-18): Such a person has nothing to gain from any action nor anything to lose by abstaining from action. They do not rely on anyone in this world for anything.
shlokaList.name: Such a person has nothing to gain from any action nor anything to lose by abstaining from action. They do not rely on anyone in this world for anything.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
naiva tasya kṛitenārtho nākṛiteneha kaśhchana
na chāsya sarva-bhūteṣhu kaśhchid artha-vyapāśhrayaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.18 (3.17 to 3.18)

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.19: Therefore one should continuously perform their duties without attachment to the results. By performing one’s duties without attachment to the outcomes, one attains the supreme state.
shlokaList.name: Therefore one should continuously perform their duties without attachment to the results. By performing one’s duties without attachment to the outcomes, one attains the supreme state.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
tasmād asaktaḥ satatam kāryam karma samāchara
asakto hyācharan karma param āpnoti pūruṣhaḥ
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.19

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.20: Janaka and other kings achieved perfection simply by performing their duties while remaining unattached to the results of their actions. Similarly, you should perform your duties solely for the well-being of the world.
shlokaList.name: Janaka and other kings achieved perfection simply by performing their duties while remaining unattached to the results of their actions. Similarly, you should perform your duties solely for the well-being of the world.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
karmaṇaiva hi sansiddhim āsthitā janakādayaḥ
loka-saṅgraham evāpi sampaśhyan kartum arhasi
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.20

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.21: Whatever actions a great personality performs, others also emulate those actions. The entire world follows the example set by such individuals.
shlokaList.name: Whatever actions a great personality performs, others also emulate those actions. The entire world follows the example set by such individuals.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yad yad ācharati śhreṣhṭhas tat tad evetaro janaḥ
sa yat pramāṇam kurute lokas tad anuvartate
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.21

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.22: O Partha! There is no duty left for me to perform in all the three worlds nor is there anything unattained that I need to achieve. Nevertheless, I continue to engage in performing My duties.
shlokaList.name: O Partha! There is no duty left for me to perform in all the three worlds nor is there anything unattained that I need to achieve. Nevertheless, I continue to engage in performing My duties.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
na me pārthāsti kartavyam triṣhu lokeṣhu kiñchana
nānavāptam avāptavyam varta eva cha karmaṇi
shlokaList.title: Shloka 3.22

shlokaList.description: Shloka 3.23 (23-24): O Partha! If I were to refrain from carefully performing My duties, all human beings would follow my example in all respects.
shlokaList.name: O Partha! If I were to refrain from carefully performing My duties, all human beings would follow my example in all respects.
shlokaList.sanskritText:
yadi hyaham na varteyam jātu karmaṇyatandritaḥ
mama vartmānuvartante manuṣhyāḥ pārtha sarvaśhaḥ
